content.problemStatement:
Chef is playing badminton today. The service rules of this singles game of badminton are as follows:


The player who starts the game serves from the **right** side of their court.
Whenever a player wins a point, they serve next.
If the server has won an **even** number of points during a game, then they will serve from the **right** side of the service court for the subsequent point.
Chef will be the player who begins the game.

Given the number of points PPP obtained by Chef at the end of the game, please determine how many times Chef served from the **right** side of the court.

Please see the sample cases below for explained examples.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line containing one integer PPP, the points obtained by Chef.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the number of times Chef served from the **right** side of the court.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer P, the number of points Chef scored at the end of the game.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of times Chef served from the right side of the court.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each P we count even scores from 0 to P inclusive.
P=2 → scores {0,2} → 2 times.
P=9 → scores {0,2,4,6,8} → 5 times.
P=53 → floor(53/2)+1 = 26+1 = 27.
P=746 → 746/2+1 = 373+1 = 374.
P=0 → only the initial serve from right → 1.\nP=1 → even scores {0} → 1.\nP=7 → even scores {0,2,4,6} → 4.

solution.approach: Chef serves from the right side whenever his current score is even (including 0). The scores at which Chef serves from the right are 0, 2, 4, ..., up to the largest even number not exceeding P. The count of such numbers is floor(P/2) + 1. For each test case we compute ans = P/2 + 1 using integer division.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
